hook: You already know that triangles have sides and angles — but hidden inside every triangle is a rich web of special points, lines, and relationships just waiting to be uncovered. So far, you've studied triangles from the outside; this unit takes you inside, revealing the secret structure that holds them together. What's the most 'balanced' point in a triangle, and why do engineers and designers care about finding it?
concept: Every triangle contains a set of special segments and points whose properties constrain and connect the side lengths and angles in precise, predictable ways — a idea captured by the formal phrase 'triangle concurrency and inequality.'

topic_roadmap.description: You will learn how the segment connecting two midpoints of a triangle is always parallel to the third side and exactly half its length, unlocking a powerful shortcut for finding unknown measures.

Discover how every point on a perpendicular bisector is equidistant from the segment's endpoints, and how every point on an angle bisector is equidistant from the angle's sides, then use these facts to solve problems.
Master how the three medians of any triangle meet at the centroid — the triangle's 'balancing point' — located exactly $ rac{2}{3}$ of the way along each median from vertex to midpoint.
Apply the Triangle Inequality Theorem to determine which combinations of three side lengths can actually form a triangle, and to compare side lengths with their opposite angles.

Use the Hinge Theorem to compare side lengths or angles across two triangles that share two equal sides, and master the strategy of indirect proof to establish geometric truths by assuming the opposite.

Construct rigorous proofs that the perpendicular bisectors, angle bisectors, medians, and altitudes of a triangle each meet at a single point, cementing your understanding of why triangle concurrency is not a coincidence.
